name: Hardware Scavenger Hunt
description: Learners explore devices to identify internal and external hardware components. Using a labeled diagram, they locate and name components like CPU, RAM, storage, ports, and peripherals. Develops understanding that devices contain different parts with different functions.
learning_objectives: Identify common hardware components
Understand that devices contain different parts
Recognize that different components serve different functions
Use vocabulary to describe what they observe
variations: External only: focus on ports, buttons, and visible features for younger learners
Teardown activity: take apart an old device to see internal components (with supervision)
Comparison: compare hardware inside phone vs. laptop vs. tablet

name: How the Internet Works: Packet Relay Race
description: Hands-on activity where learners physically simulate how data travels across the internet. Groups represent routers, messages are broken into 'packets', and teams relay packets to destination. Afterward, learners map their path and discuss how real internet routing works.
learning_objectives: Understand that large messages are broken into packets
Recognize that packets may take different paths to destination
Understand the role of routers in directing traffic
Compare physical relay to digital packet transmission
variations: Add packet corruption: some packets are damaged and must be resent
Add congestion: simulate network traffic with many packets
Chain order: packets arrive out of order and must be reassembled

name: Network Mapping Activity
description: Learners draw maps of networks they're connected to: home network (devices connecting to router), school network (classrooms and servers), internet (their location in the global network). Discusses how information travels from device to device.
learning_objectives: Understand that networks connect multiple devices
Recognize different types of networks (home, school, internet)
Trace paths that data might take
Understand the role of routers and servers
variations: Trace a journey: follow how data travels from their device to a website and back
Compare networks: map home vs. school vs. public wifi networks
Add latency: mark how long data takes to travel between points

name: Troubleshooting Scenarios
description: Learners receive scenarios describing technology problems and must propose solutions using a systematic troubleshooting approach: identify the problem, gather information, propose solutions, test, and document results. Builds problem-solving skills beyond 'turn it off and on again'.
learning_objectives: Apply systematic troubleshooting approach
Gather relevant information about a problem
Propose multiple possible solutions
Understand when to seek professional help
variations: Real device problems: troubleshoot actual devices in classroom
Written vs. demonstrated: students write solutions vs. show them step-by-step
Network problems: focus on connectivity issues (wifi, internet, device connection)

name: Search Engine Experiments
description: Learners run the same search query on multiple search engines and compare results. They investigate how search algorithms work, how results are ranked, and how sponsored results appear. Builds understanding that search engines make decisions about what to show.
materials: Sample search queries on different topics
learning_objectives: Understand that search algorithms rank results
Recognize sponsored results vs. organic results
Understand that different search engines may return different results
Recognize that no search engine shows everything
variations: Advanced searches: use operators like AND, OR, NOT, site: to narrow results
Topic bias: search divisive topics and notice how results differ by engine and region
Personalization: log in with different accounts and compare personalized results

name: Source Credibility Evaluation Challenge
description: Learners evaluate the credibility of various sources using criteria: author expertise, publication reputation, currency, bias, and citations. They rate sources from very credible to questionable and discuss their reasoning. Includes legitimate sources and deliberate misinformation.
materials: Sample sources (articles, websites, videos, social posts)
learning_objectives: Apply multiple criteria to evaluate credibility
Recognize bias and determine if it compromises reliability
Understand author expertise and credentials
Distinguish between credible and questionable sources
variations: Peer review: students propose sources and peers evaluate them
Current events: evaluate sources discussing real news stories
Deep dive: research one source fully to understand its reputation and bias

name: Fact-Check Investigation Lab
description: Learners investigate whether specific claims are true, false, or somewhere in between. They use fact-checking websites, cross-reference sources, and trace claims to original sources. Develops skills for evaluating viral claims and urban legends.
materials: Claims cards (mix of true, false, partly true)
learning_objectives: Use fact-checking websites and tools
Trace claims to original sources
Understand that claims can be partly true or misleading even if technically accurate
Recognize common fact-checking challenges
variations: Viral claims: investigate trending claims on social media
Historical misinformation: research false claims that spread widely in the past
Create-your-own: students write plausible-sounding false claims for peers to investigate

name: Misinformation Pattern Analysis
description: Learners analyze examples of misinformation and disinformation to identify common tactics: emotional manipulation, false authority, oversimplification, missing context, and appeal to tribal identity. Helps them recognize these patterns in future encounters.
materials: Examples of misinformation from various sources
learning_objectives: Identify common misinformation tactics
Recognize emotional manipulation and appeals to identity
Understand how context is used to mislead
Predict how similar tactics might appear in different contexts
variations: Historical analysis: examine famous misinformation campaigns
Media creation: students create exaggerated or misleading content to demonstrate tactics
Vulnerability assessment: reflect on which tactics they find most convincing

name: Professional Email Writing Workshop
description: Learners study examples of professional and unprofessional emails, then write emails appropriate to different contexts and audiences. They learn about subject lines, tone, formatting, and when to use email vs. other communication. Develops practical communication skills.
learning_objectives: Compose clear, concise email messages
Choose appropriate tone for different audiences
Write effective subject lines
Format and organize email content logically
Know when email is appropriate communication method
variations: Role play: email to teachers, employers, peers, customer service
Peer review: exchange drafts and provide feedback using rubric
Problem-solving: rewrite poor emails to make them professional

name: Collaborative Document Creation Project
description: Learners work together using Google Docs, Office 365, or similar to create a shared document. They practice leaving comments, suggesting changes, managing versions, and coordinating their work. Develops collaborative and project management skills essential in modern work.
learning_objectives: Use collaborative document tools effectively
Leave constructive comments and suggestions
Manage version control and document history
Coordinate work with multiple contributors
Give and receive feedback on shared work
variations: Asynchronous collaboration: team members work at different times
Real-time collaboration: all work simultaneously and see changes
Project types: research paper, presentation, shared syllabus

name: Password Strength Tester
description: Learners use online tools to test password strength and understand why some passwords are stronger than others. They learn about length, complexity, uniqueness, and common mistakes. Creates passwords and tests them. Builds understanding of how passwords protect accounts.
learning_objectives: Understand what makes a password strong
Create passwords that are both secure and memorable
Recognize common weak password patterns
Understand the relationship between password strength and account security
variations: Time-to-crack: discuss how long it would take to guess different passwords
Passphrase method: explore using memorable phrases as passwords
Password manager: research how password managers help with secure passwords

name: Privacy Audit Activity
description: Learners examine privacy settings on social media accounts, apps, and devices. They identify what information is public, private, or shared with third parties. They make intentional choices about what information to share. Develops awareness of digital footprint.
learning_objectives: Locate and understand privacy settings
Identify what information is public vs. private
Understand data-sharing practices of platforms
Make conscious choices about information sharing
Understand permanence of information shared online
variations: Parent involvement: audit privacy settings together as family
Data download: request and examine data that companies have collected
Policy analysis: read privacy policies and discuss implications

name: Cyberbullying Response Scenarios
description: Learners respond to realistic cyberbullying scenarios. They discuss what happened, why it's harmful, who should be involved, what the target should do, what bystanders should do, and how to prevent. Develops empathy and practical response skills.
learning_objectives: Recognize cyberbullying in various forms
Understand harm caused by cyberbullying
Plan supportive responses
Understand the role of bystanders
Know resources for targets
variations: Role play: act out scenarios and practice responses
Different contexts: cyberbullying in gaming, social media, texting, comments
Historical case studies: examine real cyberbullying cases and outcomes

name: Digital Footprint Analysis
description: Learners search for themselves online to discover what information is publicly available. They identify embarrassing posts, photos, or information and discuss implications. They plan how to manage their digital presence. Develops awareness of permanence and searchability.
learning_objectives: Understand what information is discoverable about them
Recognize consequences of past online actions
Understand the permanence of online information
Plan strategies to manage digital presence
Recognize implications for future opportunities
variations: Future perspective: discuss how digital footprint affects college/job applications
Positive footprint: plan what you want to be searchable
Removal: research how to request removal of embarrassing content

name: Scam Identification Challenge
description: Learners receive examples of common scams: phishing emails, fake websites, too-good-to-be-true offers, and fake customer service. They identify red flags and discuss why they work. Develops awareness of manipulation tactics.
learning_objectives: Recognize common online scams
Identify red flags (urgency, requests for money/info, too-good-to-be-true)
Understand how scams manipulate psychology
Know appropriate responses (report, don't engage, tell adults)
variations: Create-your-own: students design realistic scams (without sharing how to execute them)
Generational differences: show older and younger target scams
Prevention: plan how to protect account against phishing

name: Algorithm Analysis: How Recommendations Work
description: Learners examine how recommendation algorithms work in Netflix, Spotify, YouTube, and social media. They track what they watch/listen/interact with and notice patterns in recommendations. They discuss whether recommendations are helpful or whether algorithms trap them in echo chambers.
materials: Access to various platforms with recommendations
learning_objectives: Understand that algorithms personalize content
Recognize that algorithms are designed to maximize engagement
Understand concepts of echo chambers and filter bubbles
Evaluate whether personalization is helpful or limiting
variations: Comparative study: clear history and see what recommendations change
Deliberate deviation: intentionally watch different types of content and observe recommendations
Multiple users: compare recommendations across different accounts

name: Algorithmic Bias Detective
description: Learners investigate examples of algorithmic bias: hiring tools that discriminate, facial recognition that fails on certain groups, loan algorithms that perpetuate inequality. They discuss why bias occurs and what fairness might look like.
learning_objectives: Understand that algorithms can perpetuate human bias
Recognize bias in training data, algorithm design, and outcomes
Understand concept of fairness in algorithmic systems
Recognize that 'objective' algorithms can have subjective effects
variations: Real-world impact: research actual lawsuits related to algorithmic bias
Design challenge: propose ways to make algorithms more fair
Interactive tools: use online bias detection tools

name: Data Collection Tracking Activity
description: Learners track what data is collected about them for one day: websites visited, apps used, searches performed, location data, purchases, social media interactions. They identify who is collecting data and what it's being used for. Develops awareness of data practices.
learning_objectives: Identify what data is collected in everyday activities
Understand who has access to personal data
Recognize that data collection is often invisible
Understand how data is used for personalization and profit
variations: Multiple days: extend tracking over a week to notice patterns
Device comparison: compare data collection across phone, computer, tablet
Opt-out challenge: attempt to reduce data collection and document what's possible

name: AI Experiment & Testing
description: Learners interact with AI systems (chatbots, image generation, translation, game AI) and explore their capabilities and limitations. They ask it tricky questions, test biases, and discuss when AI works well vs. poorly.
materials: Access to various AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, DALL-E, Google Translate, etc.)
learning_objectives: Understand what AI can and cannot do
Recognize AI limitations and failure modes
Understand that AI training reflects training data
Evaluate claims about AI capabilities critically
variations: Comparative testing: test same question across multiple AI systems
Adversarial testing: deliberately try to make AI fail or act inappropriately
Bias testing: ask AI about sensitive topics and examine bias

name: Data-Driven Claim Evaluation
description: Learners evaluate claims that use data as evidence. They examine graphs, statistics, and study results. They learn to spot misleading graphs, understand correlation vs. causation, and recognize when data supports or doesn't support claims.
materials: Collection of data-driven claims (news articles, studies, misleading graphs)
learning_objectives: Understand difference between correlation and causation
Recognize misleading graphs and statistics
Evaluate whether data actually supports the claimed conclusion
Understand limitations of data and studies
variations: Misleading graphs: identify what's misleading in graphs and suggest improvements
Research paper analysis: evaluate a simplified academic study
Statistical literacy: understand confidence intervals, p-values, and study limitations
